# UC-22.7.10 · Enterprise Risk Appetite vs Open Critical Vulnerabilities

## Description

Compares counts of open critical findings against a documented risk tolerance threshold so risk management strategy is operationalized, not only slide-deck text.

## Value

Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST CSF compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes vuln and sourcetypes tenable:vuln. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.

## Implementation

(1) Set max_critical_hosts in lookup from risk committee; (2) normalize hostnames; (3) alert on breach=1; (4) document exceptions with compensating controls. Indexes required: `index=vuln`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=tenable:vuln`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=vuln` `sourcetype="tenable:vuln"` (severity, host); `risk_appetite_thresholds.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Set max_critical_hosts in lookup from risk committee; (2) normalize hostnames; (3) alert on breach=1; (4) document exceptions with compensating controls.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vuln severity=Critical earliest=-7d
| stats dc(host) as affected_hosts, dc(cve) as open_cves
| append [| inputlookup risk_appetite_thresholds.csv | fields max_critical_hosts | head 1 | eval join=1 ]
| stats max(affected_hosts) as ah max(max_critical_hosts) as lim
| eval breach=if(ah>lim,1,0)
```

Understanding this SPL

**Enterprise Risk Appetite vs Open Critical Vulnerabilities (GV.RM-01)** — Compares counts of open critical findings against a documented risk tolerance threshold so risk management strategy is operationalized, not only slide-deck text.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=vuln` `sourcetype="tenable:vuln"` (severity, host); `risk_appetite_thresholds.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: vuln; **sourcetype**: tenable:vuln. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=vuln, sourcetype=tenable:vuln, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` aggregates the pipeline (counts, distinct values, sums, percentiles, etc.) into fewer rows.
• Appends rows from a subsearch with `append`.
• `stats` aggregates the pipeline (counts, distinct values, sums, percentiles, etc.) into fewer rows.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **breach** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.severity, Vulnerabilities.signature, Vulnerabilities.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Enterprise Risk Appetite vs Open Critical Vulnerabilities (GV.RM-01)** — Compares counts of open critical findings against a documented risk tolerance threshold so risk management strategy is operationalized, not only slide-deck text.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=vuln` `sourcetype="tenable:vuln"` (severity, host); `risk_appetite_thresholds.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Single value, Table

## SPL

```spl
index=vuln sourcetype=tenable:vuln severity=Critical earliest=-7d
| stats dc(host) as affected_hosts, dc(cve) as open_cves
| append [| inputlookup risk_appetite_thresholds.csv | fields max_critical_hosts | head 1 | eval join=1 ]
| stats max(affected_hosts) as ah max(max_critical_hosts) as lim
| eval breach=if(ah>lim,1,0)
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.severity, Vulnerabilities.dest, Vulnerabilities.signature | sort - count
```

## Visualization

Single value, Table. Key fields: `affected_hosts`, `ah`, `breach`, `join`, `lim`, `open_cves`.
